steps.title: Push. Move. Transfer energy.
steps.body:
You're about to push a block and watch energy move in real time.

**Work** is energy transferred by a force over a distance. No friction? Every joule goes straight into motion. That's the deal:

steps.title: Putting a number on work
steps.body:
When a force pushes in the same direction something moves:

**F** is the force (newtons, N). **d** is the distance it moves (meters, m). Multiply them and you get work, measured in **joules (J)**.

steps.prompt: You push a box with the same force, but slide it twice as far. How much work do you do?
hints: W = F·d. The force didn't change, only the distance.

steps.prompt: Plug in the numbers. You push a block with **F = 10 N** across **d = 2 m**. Drag each value into its spot, then find the work.
steps.fillHint: Force goes in the F spot, distance in the d spot. Here F = 10 and d = 2.

steps.title: Where did the energy go?
steps.body: With no friction, all that work lands in one place: the block's **motion**. The net work equals the change in kinetic energy. That's the **work-energy theorem**.

steps.prompt: Same push — no friction. By the work-energy theorem, how much did the block's kinetic energy change? Drag **ΔKE**.
hints: W_net = ΔKE. You already found W_net.
If 20 J of work went into the block, ΔKE = 20 J.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You sized work and kinetic energy change — and saw that W_net = ΔKE every time.
